title: SEGMENT 3 — Sulston's cell lineage: the cell-by-cell movie of the worm

lines.text: SEGMENT 3 — Sulston's cell lineage: the cell-by-cell movie of the worm
So you have a worm.
You have an embryo.
The embryo is a single fertilized egg, sitting in a thin transparent shell, about fifty micrometers long.

Over the next fourteen hours, that single cell will divide, and divide, and divide, and the resulting cells will move, differentiate, organize themselves, and become a larval worm that hatches out of the shell.
Then, over the next two and a half days, that larval worm will grow, divide further, lay down its adult anatomy, and become a reproductive adult with 959 somatic cells.
The question that John Sulston asked, starting in the mid-1970s, was: what if we wrote down, cell by cell, the entire family tree of how every adult cell descended from the single fertilized egg?
Let me say that again, because the audaciousness of the question is easy to miss.

Sulston's goal was to construct, for the worm, a complete cell-lineage diagram — a chart, like a family tree, showing for every one of the 959 adult cells which cell it came from, which cell came from before that, all the way back to the fertilized egg.
Every division.
Every cell death.
Every migration.
The complete, exhaustive, cell-by-cell movie of how a worm gets built.
The analogy: the Sulston lineage is the cell-by-cell movie of how every adult cell came from a fertilized egg.
An absurdly heroic feat of microscopy and patience.
Sulston, working with collaborators including Einhard Schierenberg and John White, sat at a microscope for hours every day, for years, watching individual worm embryos develop.
He drew diagrams.
He named cells.
He tracked their daughters.

He noted which cells died — because in worms, exactly 131 cells are produced during development and then deliberately killed by programmed cell death, a tightly stereotyped process that Sulston also catalogued.
He produced, eventually, a chart that looks like a phenomenally detailed family tree, with the fertilized egg at the top and the 959 adult cells at the bottom, every connection drawn in.
Why was this such a big deal?
Two reasons, one biological and one technical.
The biological reason: the worm cell lineage is invariant.
Every worm goes through exactly the same divisions, in exactly the same order, producing exactly the same cells in exactly the same positions, every single time.
That's astonishing.
In a mouse or a human embryo, no two embryos are wired exactly the same way.
There's stochasticity in cell division, in cell migration, in cell fate choice.
Two genetically identical mouse embryos develop into mice with slightly different brain wiring, slightly different organ structure.
In the worm, that's not true.
Two worm embryos, grown side by side, will produce two worms whose cells are arranged in the same positions, dividing in the same order, dying at the same times, doing the same jobs.
The worm is, in a sense, a deterministic developmental program.
The cell lineage is the trace of that program.
Reading the lineage is like reading the source code.
The technical reason: once you have the lineage, you can ask, for any mutant worm, exactly which cell did something wrong.
If a worm with a particular mutation lacks a certain neuron, you can look at the lineage and ask, "Which division should have produced that neuron?
Did the division happen?
Did it produce the right daughter cells?
Did one daughter die when it should have lived?
" You can pinpoint, at single-cell resolution, the developmental step at which the mutation acted.
That's a level of precision in developmental analysis that simply cannot be achieved in any other animal.
Sulston's lineage was published in pieces through the late 1970s and early 1980s, and the complete adult lineage appeared in a landmark 1983 paper.
It is, to this day, the only complete cell-lineage map of any multicellular animal.
No other organism has been mapped this thoroughly.

The worm is unique not just because Brenner picked it, but because Sulston had the patience to do this work — to sit at a microscope for what amounted to a decade and draw, by hand, the developmental program of an animal.
Alongside the lineage came the discovery of programmed cell death, or apoptosis, as a stereotyped developmental phenomenon.
Sulston noticed that of the cells produced during worm development, exactly 131 were always killed — and always the same 131, in the same lineages, at the same developmental times.
The deaths were not accidents.
They were part of the program.

Subsequent genetic work, primarily by Robert Horvitz, identified the genes that controlled this death program — ced-3, ced-4, ced-9, and others — and showed that those genes have direct mammalian homologs that control apoptosis in humans, including in cancer (where apoptosis is often dysregulated) and in neurodegenerative disease (where apoptosis is often inappropriately activated).
The molecular logic of cell death, in your body right now, was worked out in worms.

The Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine for 2002 went jointly to Sydney Brenner, John Sulston, and Robert Horvitz, "for their discoveries concerning genetic regulation of organ development and programmed cell death.
" It was, in a real sense, the Nobel for choosing the worm and then doing what the worm made possible.
Brenner had picked it.
Sulston had mapped it.
Horvitz had used it to find the cell-death genes.
Three Nobel laureates from one nematode and one decision in 1965.
The big idea to leave this segment with: the worm cell lineage is the first and only complete cellular ancestry of a whole animal.
It set the standard for what developmental biology could aspire to — single-cell resolution, complete coverage, deterministic mapping.
Modern single-cell transcriptomics, which we'll touch on in Segment 18, is in some sense an attempt to replicate the Sulston-style level of resolution in organisms much larger than the worm.
We're not there yet for the mouse or human.
But the worm has been there for forty years.
Now let's open up how you do genetics in this system — how you find which gene does which job, when you have a transparent worm with a complete cell map and a freezer full of mutant strains.
